#ArtemisII yesterday was incredible, it was revealing for a lot of things. 

One thing that stands out clearly is the difference in the display and communication to the public between @NASA and @SpaceX  - to be clear I love both of these institutions and this is from an intent to provide feedback in a positive way so that @NASA can continue inspiring generations of curious minds like my own. 

The big one that stands out was the broadcast differences, the clear communication and "Explain like I'm 5" language of the SpaceX team is unparalleled, this helps new people that are curious learn, be more eager and feel "involved" more, especially as new people are eager to be apart of things.

Camera's... my goodness. During the stream, I remember the booster separation was about to happen, and some genius thought that was the best time to look at the crowd instead of the booster separation. I seriously shouted at the TV, including my wife even who is not a space nerd by any means "Are you kidding me?!" 

Then there is the actual camera quality/streaming quality, I watched on @YouTube , why it wasn't streamed also on @X is beyond my understanding, but its clear that @NASAArtemis could leverage @Starlink  to provide better amazing communication capabilities not only to the astronauts when they are/were orbiting and during the launch, but also to the viewers... who by the way of popular opinion help sway politicians to influence @NASA's budget. ;-) - MAKE THE PEOPLE SEE MORE!

The broadcast was all over the place like I said, to ground-based telescopes, to poor camera frame-rates, to randomly showing people during pivotal moments of the launch, it was all over. This is @NASA for crying out loud in the year 2026, this should've been dramatically more polished, higher quality, more accessible than it was.  

Watch a @SpaceX  broadcast, even their Falcon 9 missions (Which I am not suggesting are not special, just more common placed) are very polished, have a beautiful UI that the viewers see, the actual rocket, telemetry, altitude, speed, etc.... - there eventually was one for the Artemis broadcast, but it was laggy, super dark. difficult to see, and when the solar panels opened, the model did not reflect that.

🚀 Did you know the road to the Moon starts on the rails? The massive solid rocket boosters for #ArtemisII traveled nearly 2,800 miles by train to reach the launchpad. 🛤️🌕 

Manufactured by @NorthropGrumman in Promontory, Utah, these 10 booster segments are too heavy for planes and too big for most highways. Rail is the ONLY way to move 180-ton rocket motors safely across 8 states. #SpaceRail #NASA 

The journey ended in Florida at the Jay Jay Rail Yard, where the @FloridaEastCoast Railway handed them off to @NASAGroundSys. From there, specialized "Shuttlewagons" moved them into the Kennedy Space Center for stacking. 🚂✨

Next time you see a freight train, remember: it might just be carrying the next giant leap for humanity. Godspeed to the crew of Artemis 2! 🇺🇸🇨🇦 #Artemis #Railroads #FutureOfSpace

NASA provides a live tracker called the Artemis Real-time Orbit Website (AROW), available on the web and via the NASA mobile app. It shows:
•  Current speed/velocity
•  Distance from Earth and the Moon
•  Position in the trajectory
•  Mission elapsed time
You can access it here: NASA AROW tracker (or search for “NASA AROW Artemis II”). The mobile app also supports augmented reality to visualize Orion’s location relative to your position on Earth.

The Artemis 2 toilet (Orion’s Universal Waste Management System) had a jammed urine fan from a controller glitch—solid waste still worked fine.

Fix: Ground control radioed precise steps to Christina Koch. She removed panels, cleared the jam, and they remotely power-cycled it. Done in ~6 hours; now “go for use.”

Meantime: Crew used Collapsible Contingency Urinals (personal funnels + bags) for pee, vented overboard. No big drama—standard backup for deep space. 🚀

Science News:  NASA just launched Artemis 2. What happens today could make or break the moon mission  https://t.co/SXSvRxz3O9 https://t.co/0jW9XX5Mqe

The Artemis 2 mission is a critical bridge between uncrewed testing and a permanent lunar presence. Today’s focus isn't just about getting off the ground; it’s about the proximity operations and life support testing that occur shortly after launch.

Once the Orion capsule reaches a High Earth Orbit (HEO), the crew will perform a "proximity operations demonstration." This involves using the spacecraft’s cameras and sensors to manually fly near the spent Interim Cryogenic Propulsion Stage (ICPS). This maneuver is essential for proving that Orion can perform the complex docking and rendezvous tasks required for future missions that will connect with the Lunar Gateway or the Starship HLS lander.

Furthermore, this mission is the first time humans will rely on the Orion’s Environmental Control and Life Support System (ECLSS) in deep space. Unlike the International Space Station, which can be resupplied or evacuated quickly, Artemis 2 will push the crew beyond the protection of Earth’s magnetic field. Testing how the system handles the radiation environment and carbon dioxide scrubbing over a 10-day period is the "make or break" milestone for certifying the vehicle for the Artemis 3 lunar landing.

No, they wouldn't need to turn around or burn extra fuel to get back. Artemis 2 flies a free-return trajectory: after the trans-lunar injection burn from Earth orbit, the Orion spacecraft is on a path where Earth's and Moon's gravity naturally curve it around the Moon (or past it) and slingshot it home—no further propulsion required.

It's the same safety feature that saved Apollo 13. The European Service Module has plenty of delta-v for mid-course tweaks if needed, but the mission is designed to return safely even without them.

 For the two boosters on Artemis 2: approximately 400–600 million dollars.  Some analyses cite figures closer to ~290 million dollars per new-production booster (though Artemis 2 uses some heritage Shuttle-era casings with newly cast propellant, which may reduce the cost slightly compared to fully new ones). 

This is the hardware cost that is effectively "wasted" (or rather, expended) per launch because nothing is recovered for reuse.

Yes, a toilet is absolutely needed on Artemis 2. The Orion capsule is a tiny, sealed spacecraft for a ~10-day lunar flyby—no "just go outside" option in vacuum. In microgravity, waste doesn't drop; it floats. Their system uses suction, air flow, and containment to keep things hygienic and safe for the crew. NASA’s troubleshooting the glitch now to avoid any floating surprises. 🚀

You're spot on—Artemis 1's 25-day distant retrograde orbit was all about max testing: pushing Orion to its limits in a stable but extended lunar path. Artemis 2's shorter 10-day free-return is the efficient crewed shakedown—straight out, slingshot past the Moon, and back—focusing on human factors first. 

Glad you dug the Greek goddess lore! Artemis finally gets her moonshot spotlight. Apollo who? 🚀

Grok: 

- Artemis 2 follows a free-return trajectory using the Moon's gravity for a natural slingshot back to Earth, resulting in a distant flyby of roughly 4,000 miles instead of entering lunar orbit.
- Unlike Apollo 10, which orbited the Moon and tested the lunar module's low-altitude descent to 9 miles, Artemis 2 has no lander and focuses solely on validating the Orion capsule's deep-space performance and reentry.
- The path minimizes fuel use after translunar injection, reduces mission risk, allows system tests and observations during the flyby, and positions the crew to set a new record for farthest distance from Earth.

Artemis 1 (Nov 2022) was NASA's uncrewed test: SLS rocket launched Orion spacecraft on a 25-day loop around the Moon (farthest any crew-rated ship had gone), testing heat shield, navigation, and return. Success paved the way.

Artemis 2 (launched April 1, 2026) is the first crewed one: 4 astronauts (Reid Wiseman, Victor Glover, Christina Koch, Jeremy Hansen) on a 10-day free-return flyby of the Moon and back. No landing, but humans in deep space for the first time since Apollo. Next up: Artemis 3 landing. 🚀

The crew of the Artemis 2 mission consists of four astronauts, three from NASA and one from the Canadian Space Agency (CSA).
Here are the names and functions of each:
Reid Wiseman (NASA): Mission Commander.
Victor Glover (NASA): Pilot (will be the first black person to travel to the lunar neighborhood).
Christina Koch (NASA): Mission Specialist (will be the first woman to fly on a lunar mission).
Jeremy Hansen (CSA): Mission Specialist (will be the first Canadian and the first non-American to go to the Moon).
Details of the Mission
Artemis 2 is a historic landmark, being the first manned mission of the Artemis program. The goal is to perform an approximately 10-day flight around the Moon, testing all systems of the Orion capsule and the SLS rocket before the return of humans to the lunar surface on the next mission, Artemis 3.
Interestingly, today (April 1, 2026) marks the official beginning of this journey, with the launch having taken place according to NASA's schedule.

Bart Sibrel was on Joe Rogan a couple times and a few other podcasts. He has a compelling argument for the we didn't go to the moon theory. I haven't seen any comment recently on what he thinks about the Artemis launch. Grok gathered up things he has said about it. And I tend to agree with him.

Bart Sibrel, a prominent moon landing conspiracy theorist and filmmaker (known for works like *A Funny Thing Happened on the Way to the Moon*), has repeatedly argued that NASA's **Artemis program**—particularly efforts like Artemis II (a planned crewed lunar orbit mission)—serves as an attempt to rehabilitate or bolster the credibility of what he claims were the faked Apollo moon landings from the late 1960s/early 1970s.

In an April 2025 interview (republished in early 2026 amid Artemis II preparations), Sibrel described Artemis 2 as a "**new moon mission to boost credibility of fake moon-landings 54 years ago**." He questioned why NASA would pursue it "now, of all times, when AI imagery has reached a level of quality where fakes can hardly be detected," suggesting modern technology makes staging easier but also implying ongoing deception.

### Key Points from Sibrel on Artemis and Related NASA Efforts
Sibrel ties Artemis to his broader claims about Apollo:
- **Technological regression argument** — He asserts that with 55+ years of better rockets and computers, NASA’s farthest crewed missions remain only a tiny fraction of the distance to the Moon (e.g., low Earth orbit or limited tests). This, he says, proves Apollo’s claimed achievements were impossible and that "technology was greater in the past than in the future," which he calls impossible. He points to prior Orion/Artemis-related tests (like uncrewed mannequin flights through radiation belts) as evidence of limitations.
- **Radiation belts** — Sibrel claims the Van Allen radiation belts make crewed lunar travel lethal with current (or even advanced) technology, citing past NASA statements about needing to "solve the radiation problem" before sending people. He argues Artemis cannot genuinely succeed where Apollo supposedly did.
- **Destruction of evidence and past inconsistencies** — Before trusting Artemis, NASA must explain "documented fakes and inconsistencies" from Apollo, including the alleged destruction of original telemetry data, videotapes, and Apollo hardware schematics/blueprints. He views Artemis announcements as distractions from these issues.
- **Skepticism of crewed progress** — In appearances around 2025 (e.g., podcasts discussing Artemis crew press conferences), Sibrel has highlighted statements from Artemis astronauts (sometimes via edited clips criticized by others as misleading) that he interprets as admissions of technological shortcomings or that no one has been to the Moon in the modern era. He contrasts this with Apollo claims to argue ongoing inability.

Sibrel’s overarching position is that the Apollo missions were staged (with CIA involvement, per his claims), and Artemis represents another layer of NASA/government deception or a credibility-rebuilding exercise rather than genuine lunar return capability. He promotes his website (https://t.co/foTUgpvgla) for supporting materials, including videos of alleged "outtakes" and interviews.

### Context and Criticisms
These views align with Sibrel’s long-standing narrative that humans have never reached the Moon and that NASA has covered it up (including alleged murders to silence witnesses). Public reactions to Artemis milestones (like rollout or launches) often mock or anticipate Sibrel’s responses, with some calling his arguments deceptive editing or selective interpretation. Apollo astronauts and mainstream experts have directly countered him in debates, emphasizing telemetry, moon rocks, and other evidence.
